schema_json.description: An expert answer grounded in search results.

key_findings.title: Key Findings

sources_used.description: URLs of sources referenced in the answer

schema_json.title: StormAnswer

prompt:
You are a research expert being interviewed about:
RESEARCH QUESTION: What are the proven health benefits and risks of intermittent fasting in adults with metabolic syndrome?

The interviewer (Dr. Hiroshi Tanaka, Scientific perspective) asks:
QUESTION: Round 2 established that protocol type, intervention duration, sex, PA, and BMI are the dominant moderators of IF effect heterogeneity, but no snippet reported the specific I² reduction after adjustment. Two deeper methodological concerns remain: (1) risk of publication bias — are the funnel-plot/Egger's test results across the major MetS-IF meta-analyses consistent with an unbiased literature, and has any trim-and-fill or PET-PEESE correction been applied? (2) Reconciliation of the Ramadan free-living observational literature (which shows modest, reversible benefits in Muslim populations) with the supervised-RCT literature (which shows larger effects in short-term trials): is the RCT effect size inflated by intensive monitoring and selected populations, or is the Ramadan effect size deflated by behavioral confounders and measurement noise?

SEARCH RESULTS (your ONLY source of information):

  URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6571372/
  Content: A positive intercept from Egger regression indicates the meta-analysis result tend to be biased toward the right side of the funnel plot, and ...

  URL: https://www.aje.com/arc/assessing-and-avoiding-publication-bias-in-meta-analyses

  URL: https://doing-meta.guide/pub-bias
  Content: When this is the case, Egger's test indicates funnel plot asymmetry. ... In the following code, we create contour-enhanced funnel plots for both trim and fill ...

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. Provide a comprehensive, well-structured answer (200-400 words).
2. ONLY use information from the search results above. Do NOT add information not present in the sources.
3. When citing specific facts, mention which source they come from.
4. Extract 3-5 key findings as bullet points.
5. List the URLs of sources you actually used in your answer.
6. If the search results do not adequately answer the question, say so explicitly and note what information is missing.

Return a JSON object with 'answer', 'key_findings', and 'sources_used' fields.
